docs.title: Terracotta
docs.text:
Terracotta is a declarative project registry management tool for Minecraft developers. It manages registry providers like Modrinth project metadata, description, tags, and versions using a simple Gradle configuration as the single source of truth.

  • Declarative Configuration

    Define your project info, description, tags, license, and version artifacts in your build.gradle.kts.

    Getting started

  • Dry Runs (Plan)

    Preview all updates, tag changes, and version uploads before executing them, preventing incorrect uploads or metadata state drift.

    Gradle tasks

  • Maven SDK

    Integrate Terracotta's core directly into custom automation pipelines.

    Core API

  • CI/CD Integration

    Easily integrate with GitHub Actions to apply registry changes automatically when you push tags or release artifacts.

    CI/CD Guide

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. You configure Terracotta in your build.gradle.kts.
  2. Run ./gradlew terracottaPlan to view a diff of local configuration vs remote Modrinth state.
  3. Run ./gradlew terracottaApply to push changes (metadata, upload versions, synchronize tags) to the remote registry.

Example output from terracottaPlan:

~ Update summary (from: "Old summary" to: "Lightweight Paper plugin")
~ Update tags (from: ["utility"] to: ["utility", "paper"])
+ Upload version 1.2.0 (file: build/libs/my-plugin-1.2.0.jar)

docs.text:
• Fixed release tooling so each release leaves an empty "Unreleased" section in the changelog.
  • Why: Keeps the changelog ready for the next development cycle.

docs.title: CI/CD Setup with GitHub Actions
docs.text: You can automate publishing version updates and syncing project metadata using GitHub Actions.

docs.title: 1. Store your Modrinth Token
docs.text: First, add your Modrinth API token to your GitHub repository secrets: 1. Go to your repository Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions. 2. Click New repository secret. 3. Name it MODRINTH_TOKEN and paste your API token.

docs.title: 2. Create the workflow file
docs.text:
Create a workflow file in .github/workflows/deploy.yml:

name: Deploy Plugin

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - 'v*'

jobs:
  publish:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout Code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up JDK 17
        uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          distribution: 'zulu'
          java-version: '17'
          cache: 'gradle'

      - name: Deploy with Terracotta
        env:
          MODRINTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.MODRINTH_TOKEN }}
        run: ./gradlew terracottaApply

Whenever you push a tag like v1.2.0, this workflow will build your plugin and execute Terracotta to upload your release artifact automatically.

docs.title: Gradle Tasks Reference
docs.text: This reference describes the tasks provided by the Terracotta Gradle plugin.

docs.title: terracottaPlan
docs.text:
Generate semantic diffs comparing your local build configuration against all configured remote registries' project states. No changes are applied.

Usage: 

./gradlew terracottaPlan

Output Indicators: - +: Indicates a resource (e.g. version) that will be uploaded or created. - -: Indicates a resource or metadata element (e.g. tags) that will be deleted or removed. - ~: Indicates metadata fields, tags, or descriptions that will be updated on the remote registry.

docs.title: terracottaApply
docs.text:
Applies all planned operations, updating registry metadata and uploading missing versions for all configured providers.

Usage: 

./gradlew terracottaApply

Requirements: - Valid per-provider tokens configured (e.g., MODRINTH_TOKEN environment variable).

docs.title: Per-Provider Tasks
docs.text:
For each provider you configure (e.g., "modrinth", "hangar"), the plugin will register two tasks: - terracottaPlan<ProviderName> (e.g., terracottaPlanModrinth) - terracottaApply<ProviderName> (e.g., terracottaApplyHangar)

These work just like the aggregate tasks, but only operate on a single provider.

Usage: 

# Plan changes for Modrinth only
./gradlew terracottaPlanModrinth

# Apply changes for Hangar only
./gradlew terracottaApplyHangar

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
This tutorial walks you through publishing your first plugin to Modrinth using Terracotta Gradle plugin.

By the end of this tutorial, you will have:

  • Installed the Terracotta Gradle plugin
  • Configured Terracotta in your build file
  • Connected Terracotta to your Modrinth account
  • Uploaded your first release

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, you need:

  1. A Modrinth account.
  2. A Modrinth API token.
  3. A Gradle-based Minecraft plugin project.

docs.title: 1. Install Terracotta Plugin
docs.text:
Add the Terracotta plugin to your build.gradle.kts:

plugins {
    id("io.github.beduality.terracotta") version "0.1.1"
}

docs.title: 2. Configure Terracotta
docs.text:
In your build.gradle.kts, configure Terracotta with your project information and Modrinth provider:

import io.github.beduality.terracotta.core.model.TerracottaEnvironment
import io.github.beduality.terracotta.core.model.TerracottaLoader

terracotta {
    name.set("My Plugin")
    summary.set("Lightweight Paper plugin")
    description.set(file("README.md").readText())
    tags.set(listOf("paper", "utility"))
    license.set("MIT")
    gameVersions.set(listOf("1.21.8", "1.21.7"))
    loaders.set(listOf(TerracottaLoader.PAPER))
    environment.set(TerracottaEnvironment.SERVER_ONLY)

    providers {
        create("modrinth") {
            projectId.set("my-plugin") // your Modrinth project slug or ID
        }
    }
}

The versions are automatically discovered from your Gradle build (via the jar task output). You can also manually specify versions if needed.

Make sure your project contains a README.md file with your plugin description.

docs.title: 3. Configure Authentication
docs.text:
Create a Modrinth API token from your Modrinth account settings.

Set the token as an environment variable for Modrinth:

```=== "Linux / macOS"

```bash
export MODRINTH_TOKEN="your_modrinth_token"
```
Windows PowerShell 
$env:MODRINTH_TOKEN="your_modrinth_token"

Or set the token explicitly in your build.gradle.kts:

providers {
    create("modrinth") {
        projectId.set("my-modrinth-project-id")
        token.set(System.getenv("MODRINTH_TOKEN"))
    }
}

docs.title: 4. Preview the changes
docs.text:
Before uploading anything, use terracottaPlan to see what Terracotta will change:

./gradlew terracottaPlan

Terracotta will compare your local configuration with the Modrinth project and show the operations it would perform.

docs.title: 5. Publish your plugin
docs.text:
Apply the changes:

./gradlew terracottaApply

Terracotta will update your Modrinth project metadata, upload your release, and publish your compiled artifact.

docs.title: Finished
docs.text:
Your plugin is now published on Modrinth through Terracotta.

Next steps:

  • Learn about all available configuration options
  • Automate releases in CI/CD
  • Manage multiple versions and platforms

docs.text:
Add the Terracotta plugin to your build.gradle.kts:

plugins {
    id("io.github.beduality.terracotta") version "0.1.1"
}

docs.title: Snapshots (optional)
docs.text:
If you want to use snapshot versions, add the snapshot repository to your settings.gradle.kts:

pluginManagement {
    repositories {
        mavenCentral()
        gradlePluginPortal()
        maven("https://s01.oss.sonatype.org/content/repositories/snapshots/")
    }
}

Then use a snapshot version in your build.gradle.kts:

plugins {
    id("io.github.beduality.terracotta") version "0.1.0-SNAPSHOT"
}

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: This document explains the internal architecture of Terracotta and the reasoning behind key design decisions.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
Terracotta follows a separation of concerns architecture:

  1. Domain Logic: Pure, platform-agnostic business logic
  2. Registry Providers: Registry-specific implementations
  3. Build Tool Integration: Gradle plugin as the user-facing interface
  4. Infrastructure: Code for managing project infrastructure

This separation ensures that changes to one component don't require changes to others.

docs.title: Architecture Diagram
docs.text:
graph TD
    subgraph User["User (Developer)"]
        U[Developer]
    end

    subgraph GradlePlugin["terracotta-gradle-plugin"]
        GP[Terracotta Gradle Plugin]
        Plan[TerracottaPlan Task]
        Apply[TerracottaApply Task]
    end

    subgraph Core["terracotta-core (Domain Layer)"]
        PF[ProviderFactory Interface]
        DE[DiffEngine]
        SP[StateProvider Interface]
        RP[RegistryProvider Interface]
    end

    subgraph Modrinth["terracotta-provider-modrinth"]
        MPF[ModrinthProviderFactory]
        MSP[ModrinthStateProvider]
        MRP[ModrinthRegistryProvider]
    end

    U -->|Runs| GP
    GP -->|Creates| Plan
    GP -->|Creates| Apply
    Plan -->|Discovers| Core
    Apply -->|Discovers| Core
    Core -->|Uses| PF
    Core -->|Uses| DE
    Core -->|Uses| SP
    Core -->|Uses| RP
    Modrinth -->|Implements| SP
    Modrinth -->|Implements| RP
    Modrinth -->|Implements| PF
    Modrinth -.->|Implementation| Core

docs.title: The Domain Layer (terracotta-core)
docs.text: The domain layer is the heart of Terracotta. It contains:

docs.title: Canonical Data Models
docs.text:
All project data structures live in core.model:

  • TerracottaProject: The canonical project representation
  • TerracottaVersion: Version metadata and files
  • TerracottaEnvironment: Runtime environment (CLIENT, SERVER, CLIENT_SERVER)

Key principle: These models are platform-agnostic. They don't know about Modrinth, CurseForge, or any other registry.

docs.title: Provider Interfaces
docs.text:
Providers are defined as interfaces in core.provider:

  • ProviderFactory: Factory for creating registry-specific providers
  • StateProvider: Fetches remote project state
  • RegistryProvider: Applies changes to a registry

Key principle: The core layer depends only on interfaces, not implementations. This allows multiple providers.

docs.title: Diff Engine
docs.text:
The DiffEngine computes the difference between local and remote project states.

Input: Local project (always exists), Remote project (may be null)

Output: List of Operation instances describing what needs to be done

Operations: - CreateProject: Create a new project - UploadVersion: Upload a new version - UpdateMetadata: Update project name/summary/license - UpdateDescription: Update project description - UpdateTags: Update project tags

Key principle: The diff engine is pure logic. It doesn't make network calls or depend on any registry.

docs.title: The Provider Layer (terracotta-provider-modrinth)
docs.text: Each registry has its own provider implementation:

docs.title: Implementation Strategy
docs.text:
1. Implement ProviderFactory with registry-specific initialization
  2. Implement StateProvider using the registry's API client
  3. Implement RegistryProvider to apply changes via the registry's API

docs.title: Modrinth Provider Details
docs.text:
The Modrinth provider uses:

  • Ktor Client: HTTP client for API requests
  • Kotlinx Serialization: JSON serialization for requests/responses
  • API Documentation: Modrinth API Docs

docs.title: Provider Discovery
docs.text:
Providers are discovered via Java's ServiceLoader mechanism:

  1. Provider implementations register themselves via META-INF/services
  2. The Gradle plugin loads all available providers at runtime
  3. Users select a provider by name in their Gradle configuration

Key principle: New providers can be added without modifying core code.

docs.title: The Gradle Plugin (terracotta-gradle-plugin)
docs.text: The Gradle plugin provides the user-facing interface:

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
Tasks: - terracottaPlan: Compute what needs to be changed (read-only) - terracottaApply: Apply changes to registries

Provider Selection: - Users configure which providers to use - The plugin discovers available providers via ServiceLoader - Multiple providers can be used simultaneously

Environment Support: - CLIENT_ONLY: Only client-side content - SERVER_ONLY: Only server-side content - CLIENT_SERVER: Both client and server content

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. User runs ./gradlew terracottaPlan
  2. Plugin loads configured providers
  3. Plugin fetches remote state from each provider
  4. Plugin compares local vs remote state using DiffEngine
  5. Plugin outputs list of operations
  6. User reviews plan, then runs ./gradlew terracottaApply
  7. Plugin applies operations to registries

docs.title: Infrastructure (terracotta-github)
docs.text:
Manages GitHub repository configuration using Pulumi:

  • Repository settings (permissions, branches, rules)
  • GitHub Actions secrets for CI/CD
  • Webhook configuration

docs.title: Separation of Concerns
docs.text:
Each layer has a single, clear responsibility:

  • Core: Business logic, no external dependencies
  • Providers: Registry integration, no business logic
  • Gradle Plugin: Build tool integration, no logic
  • Infrastructure: Code management, no application logic

docs.title: Testability
docs.text:
• Core logic can be tested without network calls
  • Providers can be tested with mocks of the core interfaces
  • Gradle plugin tests can mock provider discovery

docs.title: Extensibility
docs.text:
Adding a new registry provider requires:

  1. Create a new module
  2. Implement the three provider interfaces
  3. Register via ServiceLoader
  4. Add to build configuration

No changes to core, Gradle plugin, or other providers needed.

docs.title: Maintainability
docs.text:
• Changes to Modrinth API only affect the Modrinth provider
  • Changes to Gradle API only affect the Gradle plugin
  • Core domain logic remains stable

docs.title: Versioning and Releases
docs.text:
Versions are managed consistently across all modules:

  • Core modules published to Maven Central
  • Gradle plugin published to Gradle Plugin Portal
  • All versions tracked in gradle/libs.versions.toml

See Releasing for the release process.

docs.title: Changelog Guidelines
docs.text: Designed for projects where consumers are developers, operators, and integrators (not just end-users).

docs.title: 1. Core Principle
docs.text:
A changelog records:

What changed that affects how the system is used, integrated, run, or depended on — and why it matters.

It is not:

  • commit history
  • implementation diary
  • internal refactoring log

docs.title: 2. Audience Model (important correction)
docs.text:
"End-user" is not a useful separator.

Use impact-based audience instead, organized under these standard subheadings in the changelog:

  • Players (End-users) → players experiencing time formatting and dimension-aware messages
  • Server Administrators (Operators) → deployers, server admins, CI/CD users configuring settings/releases
  • Developers (API consumers) → library/plugin users, integrators depending on the code
  • Maintainers → contributors and future engineers maintaining the codebase

If a change affects any of these → it belongs in the changelog.

docs.title: Behavior / runtime
docs.text:
• feature changes
  • logic changes
  • defaults
  • compatibility shifts

docs.title: API / integration
docs.text:
• public API changes
  • config schema changes
  • serialization / data format changes

docs.title: Deployment / operations
docs.text:
• runtime environment changes
  • build output changes
  • installation / packaging changes
  • required tooling changes

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
• breaking changes
  • deprecations
  • version constraints

docs.title: MAY include (if externally relevant)
docs.text:
• CI/CD changes only if they affect releases or artifacts
  • build system changes only if output or compatibility changes
  • infrastructure changes only if observable externally

docs.title: MUST NOT include
docs.text:
• formatting/lint changes
  • refactors without behavioral impact
  • internal restructuring with no external effect
  • dependency updates without impact (unless security/compatibility changes)

docs.title: Recommended: "What + Why"
docs.text: Each entry should ideally contain:

docs.title: What (required)
docs.text:
• factual description of the change
  • no implementation detail unless relevant

docs.title: Why (optional but strongly recommended)
docs.text:
• intent / motivation
  • avoids ambiguity for future readers

docs.title: Optional: Technical Notes section
docs.text:
Use only when necessary:

  • CI/CD
  • build pipelines
  • internal architecture changes

docs.title: 5. Section Model
docs.text: Use structured sections:

docs.title: Added
docs.text: New capabilities

docs.title: Changed
docs.text: Behavior modifications (non-breaking or breaking depending on context)

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: Bug fixes

docs.title: Deprecated
docs.text: Features scheduled for removal

docs.title: Removed
docs.text: Deleted features

docs.title: Security
docs.text: Vulnerabilities or security-related fixes

docs.title: Infrastructure (optional)
docs.text: Only externally relevant internal system changes

docs.title: CI/CD
docs.text:
Include only if it affects:

  • release process
  • artifacts
  • runtime validation
  • production pipeline behavior

Exclude:

  • YAML refactors
  • job reordering without impact

docs.title: Build system
docs.text:
Include only if it affects:

  • output format (jar, bundle, etc.)
  • runtime compatibility
  • performance characteristics
  • supported environments

docs.title: Chore
docs.text:
Rule:

Never include unless it has external behavior impact

Otherwise it is purely internal.

docs.title: 7. "What & Why" Style Rules
docs.text:
Preferred format:

### Added

#### Players
- Locale-based time formatting  
  - Why: support heterogeneous client environments without manual configuration

Constraints:

  • "What" must be short and concrete
  • "Why" must explain user/operator benefit
  • No implementation detail unless necessary for clarity

docs.title: 8. Writing Rules
docs.text:
• No commit messages
  • No "refactored X to Y"
  • No internal class names unless externally visible
  • Avoid jargon unless it is part of public API

docs.title: 9. Breaking Changes
docs.text:
Must be explicit:

  • mark clearly as breaking
  • describe migration impact
  • avoid burying in "Changed"

docs.title: 10. Mental Checklist (fast validation)
docs.text:
Before adding an entry:

  • Does it affect usage, integration, runtime, or deployment?
  • Can a consumer observe it?
  • Does it change behavior or contracts?
  • Does it require action from users/operators?

If all "no" → do not include.

docs.title: Documentation Framework (Diátaxis)
docs.text:
Diátaxis organizes documentation into four distinct types based on user intent:

  • Tutorials → learning-oriented ("I want to learn")
  • How-to guides → task-oriented ("I want to do")
  • Reference → information-oriented ("I need to know")
  • Explanation → understanding-oriented ("I want to understand")

Each type has a strict purpose and should not mix with the others.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Tutorials are designed to teach by doing. They guide a beginner through a complete, safe, and successful first experience.

They answer:

"I want to learn how this works by building something."

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Learning-focused
  • Step-by-step execution
  • Controlled, ideal environment
  • Single clear outcome
  • No decisions or alternatives
  • No prior knowledge assumed

docs.title: 1. Title
docs.text:
Must describe the outcome:

  • "Build a minimal REST API in Node.js"

docs.title: 2. Introduction
docs.text:
• What will be built
  • Minimal prerequisites

No theory.

docs.title: 3. Steps
docs.text:
• Sequential
  • Small and executable
  • Each step produces visible progress

Example pattern:

  • Step 1: do X
  • Step 2: do Y
  • Step 3: do Z

docs.title: 4. Final result
docs.text:
• What now works
  • How to verify success

docs.title: 5. Next steps (optional)
docs.text: • Links to how-to guides or reference docs

docs.title: Writing principles
docs.text:
• Learning by doing
  • One outcome only
  • No branching paths
  • No conceptual explanations
  • Immediate feedback at each step

docs.title: Anti-patterns
docs.text:
• Mixing in reference material
  • Multiple goals in one tutorial
  • Real-world complexity
  • Optional steps everywhere
  • Theory-heavy content

docs.title: Mental model
docs.text: "Follow these steps and succeed once in a controlled environment."

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
How-to guides solve real-world tasks.

They answer:

"I want to do X."

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Problem-oriented
  • Real environment assumptions
  • Assumes basic familiarity
  • Flexible solutions
  • Single task focus
  • Outcome-driven

docs.title: 1. Title
docs.text:
Must express task:

  • "Configure HTTPS for a reverse proxy"

docs.title: 2. Problem statement
docs.text: • What the user is trying to achieve

docs.title: 3. Preconditions
docs.text:
• Required tools
  • System state
  • Permissions

Declarative only.

docs.title: 4. Steps
docs.text:
• Actionable instructions
  • May include conditional branches
  • Focus on completion, not teaching

docs.title: 5. Outcome / verification
docs.text: • How to confirm success

docs.title: 6. Variants (optional)
docs.text:
• Alternative approaches
  • Different environments

docs.title: Writing principles
docs.text:
• Solve a specific problem
  • One default path
  • Minimal necessary context
  • Real-world assumptions
  • Action-focused language

docs.title: Anti-patterns
docs.text:
• Teaching fundamentals (tutorial drift)
  • Exhaustive API listing (reference drift)
  • Deep conceptual explanation (explanation drift)
  • Over-branching
  • Missing success criteria

docs.title: Mental model
docs.text: "A recipe for solving a specific problem in a real system."

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Reference material provides precise, complete, and structured information for lookup.

It answers:

"What are the exact details of this system?"

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Exhaustive within scope
  • Highly structured
  • Information-dense
  • Neutral and objective
  • No narrative flow
  • Designed for quick lookup

docs.title: 1. Definitions
docs.text:
Exact descriptions of entities:

  • functions
  • APIs
  • commands
  • components

docs.title: 2. Parameter tables
docs.text: Field Type Required Default Description

docs.title: 3. Signatures
docs.text:
Example:

  • createUser(input: UserInput): User

docs.title: 4. Enumerations
docs.text:
• active
  • pending
  • suspended

docs.title: 5. Error catalog
docs.text: Code Meaning Condition

docs.title: Writing principles
docs.text:
• Precision over readability
  • Exhaustive coverage
  • No procedural flow
  • No conceptual explanation
  • Canonical naming only

docs.title: Anti-patterns
docs.text:
• Instructional steps
  • Conceptual essays
  • Partial coverage
  • Opinionated guidance
  • Ambiguous naming

docs.title: Mental model
docs.text: "A precise map of a system you already know you need to use."

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Explanation builds understanding of concepts and systems.

It answers:

"Why does this exist and how does it work?"

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Concept-focused
  • Reasoning-driven
  • Contextual and interpretive
  • Not exhaustive
  • Open-ended
  • Multi-perspective

docs.title: 1. Concept introduction
docs.text:
• What the concept is
  • Where it fits

docs.title: 2. Underlying model
docs.text:
• How it works internally
  • Relationships and structure

docs.title: 3. Rationale
docs.text:
• Why it exists
  • What problem it solves

docs.title: 4. Trade-offs
docs.text:
• Alternatives
  • Limitations
  • Design constraints

docs.title: 5. Conceptual connections
docs.text:
• Related ideas
  • Broader systems

docs.title: Writing principles
docs.text:
• Focus on understanding
  • Explain "why" and "how"
  • Use reasoning and relationships
  • Selective detail (not exhaustive)
  • No procedural instructions

docs.title: Anti-patterns
docs.text:
• Step-by-step instructions (tutorial drift)
  • Exhaustive listings (reference drift)
  • Surface-level definitions
  • Pure opinion or advice
  • Over-simplification

docs.title: Mental model
docs.text: "A map of ideas and relationships that explains why the system is the way it is."

docs.title: 5. Comparison Overview
docs.text: Type Question it answers Focus Structure Completeness Tutorial How do I learn this? Learning by doing Strict step-by-step Low scope, high guidance How-to How do I do X? Task completion Flexible steps Medium, task-focused Reference What are the details? Information lookup Structured data Exhaustive within scope Explanation Why/how does it work? Understanding Conceptual narrative Selective, not exhaustive

docs.title: 6. Key Separation Rules
docs.text:
• Tutorials teach, but do not solve real-world problems
  • How-to guides solve problems, but do not teach fundamentals
  • Reference documents data, but does not instruct or explain
  • Explanation clarifies understanding, but does not instruct or enumerate

docs.title: Project Management
docs.text: This page explains how Terracotta handles project planning and management.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text: This project uses a PR-driven planning model instead of external issue trackers or project boards. All planning happens through Pull Requests against the project branch.

docs.title: Why PR-Driven Planning?
docs.text:
• Transparency: Every planning decision is visible in git history
  • Context: PR discussions provide rich context for future maintainers
  • Alignment: Planning and implementation are the same workflow
  • Version Control: Planning changes are tracked like any other change

docs.title: The Project Branch
docs.text:
The project branch is a separate branch used exclusively for project management. It is checked out as a git worktree at project/ from the main branch.

This separation keeps:

  • Main branch: Focused on code and documentation
  • Project branch: Focused on planning and tasks

docs.title: TODO.md
docs.text:
Contains concrete, actionable tasks ready to be worked on.

Content:

  • Well-defined tasks with clear acceptance criteria
  • Tasks that have been groomed and prioritized
  • Tasks ready for implementation

Workflow:

  1. Open a PR against project removing the task
  2. Wait for merge (signals others the task is being worked on)
  3. Then implement and submit the PR

Rules:

  • Remove from TODO when a PR targeting project removes it
  • Do not implement before the removal PR merges (prevents duplicate work)

docs.title: BACKLOG.md
docs.text:
Contains ideas and tasks not yet ready for implementation.

Content:

  • Ideas that need more investigation
  • Tasks that require discussion or prioritization
  • Dependent work that isn't ready
  • Large features split into smaller tasks

Workflow:

  • Items stay in BACKLOG until well-defined and actionable
  • Move to TODO when ready for implementation
  • No PR is needed just to add items to BACKLOG

docs.title: project/proposals/
docs.text:
Contains proposals for larger changes that should be discussed before implementation.

Structure:

project/proposals/
├── yaml-schema-support.md
├── cli-command-rename.md
└── new-provider-interface.md

Each proposal should include:

  • The problem being solved
  • The proposed solution
  • Alternatives considered
  • Open questions or unresolved decisions

Workflow:

  1. Create a proposal file with a descriptive name
  2. Open a PR against project containing only the proposal
  3. Iterate through review until accepted or rejected
  4. Begin implementation only after acceptance

docs.title: Starting Work on a Task
docs.text:
1. Check TODO.md for an existing task
  2. If not found, add it to TODO.md (with PR against project)
  3. Open a PR against project removing the task from TODO
  4. Wait for merge (signals the task is being worked on)
  5. Implement and submit the PR

docs.title: Proposing Large Changes
docs.text:
1. Create a proposal in project/proposals/
  2. Open a PR against project containing only the proposal
  3. Iterate through review
  4. Accept/reject the proposal
  5. If accepted, create tasks in TODO.md

docs.title: Grooming the Backlog
docs.text:
1. Review BACKLOG.md periodically
  2. Move items to TODO when they become actionable
  3. Refine or split large items
  4. Remove items that are no longer relevant

docs.title: Relationship to GitHub Issues
docs.text:
This project does not use GitHub Issues. All planning and tracking happens in the project branch:

  • Bugs → Add to TODO or BACKLOG
  • Feature requests → Add to TODO or BACKLOG
  • Enhancements → Add to TODO or BACKLOG
  • Proposals → Add to project/proposals/

If you find a bug or have a feature request, open an issue on GitHub. We'll convert it to a planning item in the project branch.

docs.title: Building the Project
docs.text: This guide covers building Terracotta for development and contribution.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• JDK 21 (the project targets JVM 17 but compiles under JDK 21)
  • Gradle 8.0+

If your system default is a newer Java version, prefix commands with: 

JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk

docs.title: Compile All Modules
docs.text:
JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew build

This runs:

  • Compilation of all modules
  • All tests (unit, integration, and smoke tests)
  • Code formatting verification (Spotless)
  • Test coverage report generation

docs.title: Run Tests
docs.text: JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew test

docs.title: Generate API Documentation
docs.text:
JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew dokkaHtml

Output is available at build/dokka/html/index.html in each module.

docs.title: Format Code
docs.text: JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew spotlessApply

docs.title: Check Code Formatting (CI requirement)
docs.text: JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew spotlessCheck

docs.title: Verifying Your Build
docs.text:
Run the complete verification pipeline:

JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew clean check

This ensures:

  • All tests pass
  • Code is properly formatted
  • Test coverage meets thresholds
  • Dokka documentation builds

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: This guide shows how to contribute to Terracotta.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository on GitHub
  2. Clone your fork locally
  3. Set up upstream for fetching changes: 
    git remote add upstream https://github.com/beduality/terracotta.git

docs.title: 1. Choose a Task
docs.text:
• Check project/TODO.md for existing tasks
  • If not found, add it to TODO (with PR against project branch)
  • Open a PR against project branch removing the task from TODO
  • Wait for merge before starting implementation

docs.title: 2. Create a Branch
docs.text:
git checkout main
git pull upstream main
git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name

docs.title: 3. Make Your Changes
docs.text: Implement your changes following the Code Style Reference.

docs.title: 4. Test Your Changes
docs.text:
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk
./gradlew build

This runs: - Compilation of all modules - All tests (unit, integration, smoke) - Code formatting verification (Spotless)

docs.title: 5. Commit Your Changes
docs.text:
Follow Conventional Commits:

git commit -m "feat(core): add new feature"
git commit -m "fix(modrinth): handle edge case"

docs.title: 6. Open a Pull Request
docs.text:
• Push your branch to your fork
  • Open a PR against main branch
  • Fill in the PR template
  • Request review from maintainers

docs.title: PR Checklist
docs.text:
Before merging, a PR must:

  • [ ] Build passes: ./gradlew build
  • [ ] Tests pass: ./gradlew test
  • [ ] Spotless passes: ./gradlew spotlessCheck
  • [ ] Test coverage maintained or improved
  • [ ] Documentation updated (if applicable)

docs.title: After Approval
docs.text:
1. Merge the PR (or have a maintainer merge it)
  2. Delete your branch
  3. Update your local main: 
    git checkout main
git pull upstream main

docs.title: Questions?
docs.text:
• Join the Discord server
  • Check the Architecture Overview
  • Review the Tech Stack Reference

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: This guide covers working with Terracotta's documentation site.

docs.title: Tech Stack
docs.text:
• MkDocs: Documentation generator
  • Material for MkDocs: Theme
  • MkDocs Material Extensions: Extra features
  • uv: Python dependency management
  • mike: Versioned documentation deployment

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Install uv for Python dependency management.

docs.title: Option 1: Full Versioned Preview (Recommended)
docs.text:
Deploy the docs under a local version alias and serve:

uv run mike deploy -t "Unreleased" unreleased
uv run mike set-default unreleased
uv run mike serve

This serves the site at http://localhost:8000 with:

  • Version switching working as in production
  • Multi-version support

docs.title: Option 2: Quick Single-Version Preview
docs.text:
For a quick preview without committing to the gh-pages branch:

uv run mkdocs serve

This starts a live-reloading server at http://localhost:8000 but without multi-version support.

docs.title: Documentation Structure
docs.text:
docs/
├── index.md                        # Landing page
├── content/
│   ├── tutorials/                  # Learning-oriented guides
│   ├── how-to-guides/              # Task-oriented guides
│   ├── reference/                  # Information-oriented reference
│   └── explanation/                # Understanding-oriented content
└── hooks/                          # MkDocs build hooks
mkdocs.yml                          # Site configuration & navigation
pyproject.toml                      # Python dependencies (uv)

The content follows the Documentation Framework structure. Each page must belong to exactly one type:

  • Tutorial: Learning by doing (beginner-focused)
  • How-to guide: Solving specific tasks
  • Reference: Exhaustive information lookup
  • Explanation: Understanding concepts and "why"

docs.title: Adding a Page
docs.text:
1. Create a .md file under docs/content/ in the appropriate category
  2. Add the page to the nav section in mkdocs.yml
  3. Preview with uv run mkdocs serve

docs.title: Rebuilding All Versions
docs.text:
If you change templates, overrides, hooks, or mkdocs.yml and want to apply them across all historical versions:

uv run python scripts/redeploy_all_docs.py

This script:

  • Checks out each release tag
  • Applies the latest config and overrides from main
  • Re-deploys all versions to the local gh-pages branch using mike

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text: Documentation is automatically deployed to GitHub Pages on every push to main via .github/workflows/deploy-docs.yml. Normally, no manual deployment is needed.

docs.title: Manual Deployment (if needed)
docs.text:
# Deploy current docs
uv run mike deploy <version-alias>

# Set default version
uv run mike set-default <version-alias>

# List versions
uv run mike list

docs.title: Follow Documentation Framework
docs.text: See the Documentation Framework for detailed guidance on each type.

docs.title: Page Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Page belongs to exactly one Diátaxis type
  • [ ] Content matches the purpose of that type
  • [ ] Title reflects the type (command for how-to, concept for explanation)
  • [ ] No mixing of types in a single page
  • [ ] Code examples are tested and working
  • [ ] Links point to correct targets

docs.title: Building Documentation for Production
docs.text:
The CI/CD workflow handles production builds. The workflow:

  1. Installs Python dependencies
  2. Builds documentation with MkDocs
  3. Deploys to GitHub Pages using mike
  4. Updates the gh-pages branch

docs.title: Releasing
docs.text: This guide covers the release process for Terracotta.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Releases are performed from the Release GitHub Actions workflow. Running the release in CI makes the process safer and easier to roll back:

  • Bumping versions, changelog updates, build verification, Maven Central publishing, and git tagging all happen in one workflow.
  • If publishing to Maven Central fails, release.py automatically rolls back the local tag and commit so no tag or changelog is left behind.
  • The GitHub release and docs deployment only happen after a successful publish.
  • The workflow can be re-run or the release can be re-triggered without force-pushing or manually cleaning up.

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text:
Releases follow Semantic Versioning. The release script automatically determines the version bump:

Commit Type Version Bump fix patch (0.1.x) feat minor (0.x.0) BREAKING CHANGE or ! suffix major (x.0.0)

In wizard mode, the script displays the detected bump and suggested version as the default.

docs.title: 1. Update the Changelog
docs.text:
Before releasing, add all user-facing changes under ## [Unreleased] in CHANGELOG.md:

## [Unreleased]

### Added
#### Docs
- Added new documentation page for X

### Changed
#### Core
- Updated Y to improve Z

### Fixed
#### CLI
- Fixed issue with command ABC

Follow the guidelines in guidelines/changelog.md: - Focus on user/developer/operator impact - Group by module (Docs, Core, Gradle Plugin, Modrinth, SDK, etc.)

docs.title: 2. Trigger the Release Workflow
docs.text:
Go to Actions → Release → Run workflow. Choose:

  • bump: auto (recommended), patch, minor, major, or custom
  • version: required only when bump is custom

docs.title: Trigger from the local CLI
docs.text:
You can also start the workflow from your machine without opening the GitHub web UI. Make sure the GitHub CLI is installed and authenticated (gh auth login):

# Wizard mode (prompts for bump type and confirmation)
uv run scripts/release.py trigger

# Non-interactive trigger
uv run scripts/release.py trigger --bump auto --yes

# Custom version
uv run scripts/release.py trigger --bump custom --version 1.2.3 --yes

The command runs gh workflow run release.yml against your current branch, passing the same bump and version inputs used by the web UI.

The workflow will:

  1. Update CHANGELOG.md, gradle.properties, pyproject.toml, and uv.lock with the new version.
  2. Run ./gradlew spotlessCheck build to verify compilation and tests.
  3. Publish to Maven Central using the central-portal-publisher plugin.
  4. If publishing succeeds, commit the version changes and push the new vX.Y.Z tag.
  5. Create a GitHub release with the changelog body and JAR artifacts.
  6. Deploy documentation using deploy-docs.yml.

docs.title: 3. Distribution & Publishing
docs.text:
The following modules are published automatically via the Sonatype Central Portal:

Module Maven Coordinates Core io.github.beduality:terracotta-core Gradle Plugin io.github.beduality:terracotta-gradle-plugin Modrinth Provider io.github.beduality:terracotta-provider-modrinth

The workflow uses the central-portal-publisher Gradle plugin to:

  • Bundle all module artifacts with proper Maven repository layout
  • Sign artifacts with GPG
  • Upload the bundle to the Sonatype Central Portal
  • Automatically publish to Maven Central

docs.title: Release Secrets
docs.text:
The release workflow uses these GitHub repository secrets:

Secret Purpose SONATYPE_USERNAME Sonatype Central Portal username SONATYPE_PASSWORD Sonatype Central Portal password/token SIGNING_KEY GPG private key (ASCII-armored) SIGNING_PASSWORD GPG key passphrase

Configure these in your repository settings under Secrets and variables → Actions.

docs.title: Loading Secrets via Pulumi
docs.text:
The terracotta-github module manages GitHub Actions secrets as infrastructure. To sync secrets from your .env file into Pulumi config (which then provisions them as GitHub Actions secrets):

export PULUMI_CONFIG_PASSPHRASE="your-passphrase"
uv run scripts/load-pulumi-secrets.py
cd modules/terracotta-github && pulumi up

The PULUMI_CONFIG_PASSPHRASE env var is required for non-interactive secret encryption. Alternatively, use PULUMI_CONFIG_PASSPHRASE_FILE to point to a file containing the passphrase.

The script auto-detects which secrets are needed by parsing App.kt — no manual list to maintain.

docs.title: Manual Override / Local
docs.text:
For local testing or dry runs, you can still run release.py locally:

uv run scripts/release.py --no-publish --no-push

This will run the version bump, changelog update, and build verification without actually publishing or pushing. To see the detected version without prompts, run:

uv run scripts/release.py --yes --no-publish --no-push

To run the full release from a local machine (not recommended):

uv run scripts/release.py --yes --publish --bump auto

This requires the same SONATYPE_USERNAME, SONATYPE_PASSWORD, SIGNING_KEY, and SIGNING_PASSWORD environment variables.

docs.title: Skip Dry-Run
docs.text:
To skip the build verification step:

uv run scripts/release.py --no-dry-run --no-publish --no-push

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
If the CI workflow fails, release.py will attempt to roll back the local tag and commit automatically. If a manual rollback is needed, run:

uv run scripts/release.py rollback

This reverts the version bump, changelog changes, and tag.

docs.title: What Happens on Release
docs.text:
1. Changelog: ## [Unreleased] → ## [0.x.y] - YYYY-MM-DD
  2. Version: Updated in gradle.properties, pyproject.toml, and uv.lock
  3. Build: ./gradlew spotlessCheck build verifies the project
  4. Publish: artifacts are signed and uploaded to Maven Central
  5. Tag: the new vX.Y.Z tag is pushed to GitHub
  6. GitHub Release: created with the changelog body and JARs
  7. Docs: documentation is deployed with mike

docs.title: Smoke Testing a Release
docs.text:
Use this checklist after a release to confirm the artifacts, registry state, and public sites are all correct end-to-end.

You can run most of these checks automatically with:

uv run python scripts/smoke_test_release.py 0.1.2

Add --build-from-tag, --gradle-e2e, or --sdk-e2e to include the optional end-to-end tests (these require a local Gradle installation).

docs.title: Investigation progress for 0.1.1 and 0.1.2
docs.text: Last checked: 2026-07-10.

docs.title: Automated smoke-test results
docs.text:
Ran uv run python scripts/smoke_test_release.py <version> for both 0.1.1 and 0.1.2:

Version GitHub Release Maven Central JAR Integrity Public Sites 0.1.1 PASS PASS PASS PASS 0.1.2 PASS PASS PASS PASS

docs.title: Verified
docs.text:
• GitHub releases: v0.1.1 and v0.1.2 both exist with correct titles, tags, and 9 JAR assets each (main JARs, sources, javadoc).
  • Maven Central: all expected coordinates return HTTP 200 for both versions:
  • io.github.beduality:terracotta-core
  • io.github.beduality:terracotta-gradle-plugin
  • io.github.beduality:terracotta-provider-modrinth
  • Gradle plugin marker: io.github.beduality.terracotta:io.github.beduality.terracotta.gradle.plugin
  • Each library has .pom, .jar, -sources.jar, -javadoc.jar, .pom.asc, .jar.asc, and .module files live.
  • POM dependencies: verified.
  • terracotta-provider-modrinth declares terracotta-core as a runtime dependency.
  • Gradle plugin marker POM points to terracotta-gradle-plugin.
  • Source tree at v0.1.1:
  • gradle.properties: version = 0.1.1
  • pyproject.toml: version = "0.1.1"
  • CHANGELOG.md: ## [0.1.1] - 2026-07-10 present, ## [Unreleased] empty.
  • Release commit only touched CHANGELOG.md, gradle.properties, pyproject.toml, uv.lock.
  • Build from tag: ./gradlew spotlessCheck build succeeded at v0.1.1 (BUILD SUCCESSFUL, 37 tasks).
  • Artifact contents: downloaded JARs from Maven Central are valid and contain the expected classes.
  • terracotta-core: DiffEngine, Operation* hierarchy, TerracottaProject, etc.
  • terracotta-gradle-plugin: TerracottaPlugin, TerracottaPlanTask, TerracottaApplyTask, extension.
  • terracotta-provider-modrinth: ModrinthStateProvider, ModrinthRegistryProvider, factory.
  • Plugin descriptor present: META-INF/gradle-plugins/io.github.beduality.terracotta.properties.
  • Sources JARs contain the expected .kt source files.
  • Public sites: main docs site and all Maven Central search pages return 200 for both versions.

docs.title: Open / flagged
docs.text:
• Docs path issue: only https://beduality.github.io/terracotta/ returns 200. Sub-paths like /sdk/reference/installation/ return 404. Need to confirm whether mike deployed the version under a /0.1.1/ prefix or if the docs deploy failed/needs the trailing .html.
  • Empty javadoc JARs: both the GitHub release and Maven Central -javadoc.jar files are only 261 bytes and contain only META-INF/MANIFEST.MF. This is consistent but should be verified against the Dokka output (expected if no public KDoc was generated yet).
  • E2E Gradle plugin / SDK tests: not run yet.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
• You will need the release version, e.g. 0.1.1.
  • Have gh, curl, gpg, jar/unzip, JDK 21, and a working Gradle project ready.
  • Optional but recommended: a throwaway test project for Gradle plugin and SDK checks.

docs.title: 1. GitHub release
docs.text:
• [ ] The release exists at https://github.com/beduality/terracotta/releases/tag/v0.1.1.
  • [ ] The tag is v0.1.1 and points to the release commit. 
    gh release view v0.1.1 --json tagName,targetCommitish,url
  • [ ] The release title is Release v0.1.1.
  • [ ] The release body matches the ## [0.1.1] section from CHANGELOG.md.
  • [ ] The JAR assets are attached:
  • terracotta-core-0.1.1.jar
  • terracotta-gradle-plugin-0.1.1.jar
  • terracotta-provider-modrinth-0.1.1.jar
  • [ ] No -sources or -javadoc JARs are missing from Maven Central if expected.

docs.title: 2. Maven Central artifacts
docs.text:
Check that all three modules are live with the right version and metadata.

VERSION=0.1.1
GROUP=io.github.beduality
ARTIFACTS=(
  terracotta-core
  terracotta-gradle-plugin
  terracotta-provider-modrinth
)

for a in "${ARTIFACTS[@]}"; do
  curl -sI "https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/${GROUP//.//}/${a}/${VERSION}/${a}-${VERSION}.pom"
done

# Gradle plugin marker
MARKER_GROUP=io.github.beduality.terracotta
MARKER_ID=io.github.beduality.terracotta.gradle.plugin
curl -sI "https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/${MARKER_GROUP//.//}/${MARKER_ID}/${VERSION}/${MARKER_ID}-${VERSION}.pom"

For each artifact verify:

  • [ ] .pom is downloadable and the version is 0.1.1.
  • [ ] .jar is downloadable.
  • [ ] -sources.jar is downloadable.
  • [ ] -javadoc.jar is downloadable.
  • [ ] .pom.asc signature is present and the GPG key is the expected signing key.
  • [ ] .jar.asc signature is present.
  • [ ] .module Gradle metadata is published (for the Gradle plugin).

The 0.1.1 release was validated as 5 Maven Central components:

Artifact ID Group ID Type terracotta-core io.github.beduality library terracotta-gradle-plugin io.github.beduality library terracotta-provider-modrinth io.github.beduality library io.github.beduality.terracotta.gradle.plugin io.github.beduality.terracotta Gradle plugin marker POM

Verify the plugin marker as well:

curl -sI "https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/io/github/beduality/terracotta/io.github.beduality.terracotta.gradle.plugin/0.1.1/io.github.beduality.terracotta.gradle.plugin-0.1.1.pom"

Quick verification using Maven Central search:

  • [ ] https://central.sonatype.com/artifact/io.github.beduality/terracotta-core/0.1.1 loads.
  • [ ] https://central.sonatype.com/artifact/io.github.beduality/terracotta-gradle-plugin/0.1.1 loads.
  • [ ] https://central.sonatype.com/artifact/io.github.beduality/terracotta-provider-modrinth/0.1.1 loads.
  • [ ] https://central.sonatype.com/artifact/io.github.beduality.terracotta/io.github.beduality.terracotta.gradle.plugin/0.1.1 loads.

docs.title: 3. Source tree at the release tag
docs.text:
• [ ] The v0.1.1 tag exists in the repository. 
    git fetch --tags origin
git checkout v0.1.1
  • [ ] gradle.properties contains version = 0.1.1.
  • [ ] pyproject.toml contains version = "0.1.1".
  • [ ] CHANGELOG.md has a ## [0.1.1] - YYYY-MM-DD header and an empty ## [Unreleased] section.
  • [ ] README.md plugin sample uses version 0.1.1.
  • [ ] The build passes from the tagged tree. 
    ./gradlew spotlessCheck build

docs.title: 4. Artifact integrity
docs.text:
Download the released JARs and verify their contents.

mkdir -p /tmp/terracotta-smoke && cd /tmp/terracotta-smoke
VERSION=0.1.1
for m in terracotta-core terracotta-gradle-plugin terracotta-provider-modrinth; do
  curl -O "https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/io/github/beduality/${m}/${VERSION}/${m}-${VERSION}.jar"
  jar tf "${m}-${VERSION}.jar" | head -n 20
  jar tf "${m}-${VERSION}.jar" | wc -l
done
  • [ ] Each JAR is a valid ZIP with class files.
  • [ ] No obviously missing packages or META-INF issues.
  • [ ] The Gradle plugin JAR contains the plugin descriptor under META-INF/gradle-plugins/.
  • [ ] The provider JAR references the core classes.

docs.title: 5. Gradle plugin end-to-end
docs.text:
Create a minimal project that applies the plugin from Maven Central and runs the dry-run task.

mkdir -p /tmp/terracotta-plugin-smoke && cd /tmp/terracotta-plugin-smoke
cat > settings.gradle.kts <<'EOF'
rootProject.name = "terracotta-plugin-smoke"
EOF

cat > build.gradle.kts <<'EOF'
plugins {
    id("io.github.beduality.terracotta") version "0.1.1"
}

terracotta {
    artifactFile.set(layout.projectDirectory.file("dummy-artifact.jar"))
    providers {
        create("modrinth") {
            projectId.set("dummy-project-id")
        }
    }
    name.set("Smoke Test")
    summary.set("Smoke test project")
    description.set("Testing the 0.1.1 release.")
    license.set("MIT")
    tags.set(listOf("test"))
    gameVersions.set(listOf("1.20.1"))
    loaders.set(listOf(io.github.beduality.terracotta.core.model.TerracottaLoader.PAPER))
    environment.set(io.github.beduality.terracotta.core.model.TerracottaEnvironment.SERVER_ONLY)
}
EOF

# The plugin needs an artifact file to plan with; create a placeholder.
touch dummy-artifact.jar

Run:

./gradlew terracottaPlan --stacktrace
  • [ ] The plugin resolves from Maven Central without manual repository configuration.
  • [ ] terracottaPlan completes successfully (or fails only due to missing Modrinth token, not class/plugin errors).
  • [ ] terracottaApply can be invoked in --dry-run or token-less mode without crashes.

docs.title: 6. SDK / library end-to-end
docs.text:
Create a small Kotlin/Java project that resolves the core and provider artifacts from Maven Central.

mkdir -p /tmp/terracotta-sdk-smoke && cd /tmp/terracotta-sdk-smoke
cat > build.gradle.kts <<'EOF'
plugins {
    kotlin("jvm") version "2.3.21"
}

repositories {
    mavenCentral()
}

dependencies {
    implementation("io.github.beduality:terracotta-core:0.1.1")
    implementation("io.github.beduality:terracotta-provider-modrinth:0.1.1")
}
EOF

cat > src/main/kotlin/Smoke.kt <<'EOF'
import io.github.beduality.terracotta.core.model.TerracottaLoader
import io.github.beduality.terracotta.core.model.TerracottaEnvironment

fun main() {
    val loader = TerracottaLoader.PAPER
    val env = TerracottaEnvironment.SERVER_ONLY
    println("Resolved $loader in environment $env")
}
EOF

Run:

./gradlew build --stacktrace
  • [ ] Dependencies resolve from Maven Central.
  • [ ] The project compiles.

docs.title: 7. Documentation
docs.text:
• [ ] The docs site loads at https://beduality.github.io/terracotta/.
  • [ ] The version selector or banner shows 0.1.1.
  • [ ] The Gradle plugin installation snippet uses version "0.1.1".
  • [ ] The SDK installation page references 0.1.1.
  • [ ] The API reference links for core, gradle-plugin, and provider-modrinth load without 404s.
  • [ ] No broken internal links reported by the docs build.

docs.title: 8. Changelog and commit integrity
docs.text:
• [ ] The release commit on main matches the tag. 
    git log --oneline v0.1.1 -1
  • [ ] The commit updated gradle.properties, pyproject.toml, uv.lock, and CHANGELOG.md. 
    git show --stat v0.1.1
  • [ ] No unexpected files were modified in the release commit.

docs.title: 9. Rollback / incident readiness
docs.text:
If any check fails:

  • [ ] You know whether Maven Central published successfully (irreversible) or failed before tagging.
  • [ ] If the GitHub release is wrong but Maven Central is correct, you can edit the release or re-tag manually.
  • [ ] If the tag points to the wrong commit, run uv run scripts/release.py rollback locally and re-trigger the workflow after fixing the issue.

docs.title: 10. Final sign-off
docs.text:
• [ ] GitHub release page looks correct.
  • [ ] Maven Central has all three library artifacts, the Gradle plugin marker, sources, javadoc, and signatures.
  • [ ] Tagged source tree builds and version strings match.
  • [ ] Gradle plugin resolves and runs in a clean project.
  • [ ] SDK resolves and compiles in a clean project.
  • [ ] Documentation site reflects the new version and has no broken links.

If all boxes are checked, the 0.1.1 release is fully verified.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: This guide covers running and verifying tests in Terracotta.

docs.title: Test Types
docs.text: Terracotta has two tiers of tests.

docs.title: Unit & Integration Tests
docs.text:
Run as part of the standard build, covering:

  • Core business logic in terracotta-core
  • Provider implementations
  • Gradle plugin tasks

Do not require network access or external credentials.

docs.title: Running
docs.text: JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew test

docs.title: Test Organization
docs.text: Location Tests for modules/terracotta-core/src/test/ Core modules Provider modules Provider-specific tests modules/terracotta-gradle-plugin/src/test/ Gradle plugin

docs.title: Running Specific Tests
docs.text:
# Run tests for a specific module
./gradlew :terracotta-core:test

# Run tests with a specific tag
./gradlew test --tests "*Integration*"

docs.title: Smoke Tests
docs.text:
Verify Terracotta Gradle plugin integration against the live Modrinth API:

  • End-to-end behavior
  • Real network responses
  • Authentication flows
  • Actual command output

Excluded from default test run due to external API dependency.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: • Set MODRINTH_TOKEN environment variable to a valid Modrinth API token

docs.title: Running
docs.text:
MODRINTH_TOKEN=<your-token> JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew terracottaSmokeTest

Run smoke tests before submitting a PR that touches provider logic or anything affecting Modrinth API integration.

docs.title: Test Coverage
docs.text: Terracotta uses JaCoCo for test coverage reporting.

docs.title: Generate Coverage Report
docs.text: JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew jacocoTestReport

docs.title: View HTML Reports
docs.text:
Reports are generated at:

  • modules/terracotta-core/build/reports/jacoco/test/html/index.html
  • modules/terracotta-provider-modrinth/build/reports/jacoco/test/html/index.html
  • modules/terracotta-gradle-plugin/build/reports/jacoco/test/html/index.html

docs.title: Coverage Expectations
docs.text: Type Expectation Modified code Maintain or improve coverage Important logic paths Covered by tests Integration paths Verified by smoke tests

docs.title: Tests Fail to Run
docs.text:
1. Ensure JAVA_HOME is set to JDK 21
  2. Run ./gradlew clean test to clear cached builds
  3. Check network access if using external dependencies

docs.title: Low Coverage
docs.text:
• Run jacocoTestReport to view coverage details
  • Focus on uncovered branches and edge cases
  • Add tests for error paths

docs.title: Smoke Tests Failing
docs.text:
1. Verify MODRINTH_TOKEN is valid
  2. Check Modrinth API status
  3. Ensure project exists on Modrinth

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Write tests first for new behavior (TDD when appropriate)
  2. Run tests after changes to catch regressions
  3. Add tests for edge cases before implementing features

docs.title: Architecture Reference
docs.text: This reference documents the module structure and responsibilities in the Terracotta repository.

docs.title: Module Architecture
docs.text:
Terracotta uses a multi-module Gradle project to separate platform-agnostic business logic from specific registry providers.

modules/
├── terracotta-core/              # Pure domain library
├── terracotta-provider-modrinth/ # Modrinth provider implementation
├── terracotta-gradle-plugin/     # Gradle plugin frontend
└── terracotta-github/            # Pulumi infrastructure

docs.title: terracotta-core
docs.text:
Package: io.github.beduality.terracotta.core.*

The core SDK with canonical models, provider interfaces, and the semantic diff engine.

Characteristics: - Pure Kotlin/JVM - no external dependencies - Published to Maven Central as io.github.beduality:terracotta-core - No network, framework, or build-tool dependencies

Responsibilities: - Canonical data models (TerracottaProject, TerracottaVersion) - Provider interfaces (ProviderFactory, StateProvider, RegistryProvider) - Diff engine (DiffEngine, Operation sealed interface)

Key Components:

Component Purpose core.model.* Domain models (Project, Version, Environment, Loader, ReleaseType) core.provider.* Provider interfaces for registry integration core.diff.* Semantic diff engine and operations

docs.title: terracotta-provider-modrinth
docs.text:
Package: io.github.beduality.terracotta.provider.modrinth.*

Modrinth registry provider implementation.

Characteristics: - Depends on Ktor Client and Kotlinx Serialization - Published to Maven Central as io.github.beduality:terracotta-provider-modrinth

Responsibilities: - Implement ProviderFactory for Modrinth - Implement StateProvider to fetch project state from Modrinth API - Implement RegistryProvider to apply changes to Modrinth API

Key Components:

Component Purpose ModrinthProviderFactory Factory creating Modrinth providers ModrinthClient Low-level HTTP client for Modrinth API ModrinthStateProvider Fetches project state from Modrinth ModrinthRegistryProvider Applies operations to Modrinth

docs.title: terracotta-gradle-plugin
docs.text:
Package: io.github.beduality.terracotta.gradle.*

Build-tool integration providing tasks with Gradle DSL.

Characteristics: - Uses ServiceLoader for provider discovery - Depends on Gradle API and core modules - Published to Gradle Plugin Portal

Responsibilities: - Register Gradle plugin and extension - Create terracottaPlan and terracottaApply tasks - Discover and configure providers dynamically

Key Components:

Component Purpose TerracottaPlugin Plugin entry point TerracottaExtension DSL configuration entry point TerracottaPlanTask Computes and displays diff TerracottaApplyTask Computes and applies diff

docs.title: terracotta-github
docs.text:
Package: com.pulumi.terracotta.*

Infrastructure management using Pulumi.

Characteristics: - Uses Pulumi Java/Kotlin SDK - Manages GitHub repository configuration - Not published to Maven Central

Responsibilities: - Manage repository settings and permissions - Configure GitHub Actions secrets - Set up repository rules and webhooks

docs.title: Provider Discovery
docs.text:
Providers are discovered at runtime via Java's ServiceLoader:

  1. Provider modules register implementations in META-INF/services/
  2. The Gradle plugin loads all available ProviderFactory instances
  3. Users configure which providers to use in their Gradle DSL

docs.title: Package Dependencies
docs.text: Module Depends On terracotta-core Standard Kotlin/Java APIs only terracotta-provider-modrinth terracotta-core, Ktor Client, Kotlinx Serialization terracotta-gradle-plugin terracotta-core, Gradle API terracotta-github Pulumi SDK

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text:
• Core modules published to Maven Central
  • Gradle plugin published to Gradle Plugin Portal
  • All versions tracked in gradle/libs.versions.toml

docs.title: Code Style Reference
docs.text: This reference documents the code style and formatting requirements for Terracotta.

docs.title: Basics
docs.text:
• Language Version: Kotlin 2.3.21
  • Target JVM: 17
  • Compiler: JDK 21

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text:
• Tool: ktlint via Spotless 6.25.0
  • Run: ./gradlew spotlessApply (auto-fix)
  • Check: ./gradlew spotlessCheck (CI requirement)

Configuration: Found in build.gradle.kts under spotless block.

docs.title: Conventions
docs.text:
1. Imports: No wildcard imports (import package.*)
  2. Line Length: Max 120 characters
  3. Braces: K&R style
  4. Spacing: 4 spaces, no tabs
  5. Naming:
  6. Classes: PascalCase
  7. Functions: camelCase
  8. Constants: UPPER_SNAKE_CASE
  9. Types: PascalCase

docs.title: Annotations
docs.text:
• Use @Serializable from Kotlinx Serialization for data models
  • Use @SerialName for explicit JSON property names

docs.title: Conventional Commits
docs.text:
Terracotta follows Conventional Commits.

Format: <type>(<scope>): <description>

Types:

Type Purpose Version Bump feat New feature Minor fix Bug fix Patch docs Documentation None refactor Code refactoring None test Test additions/changes None chore Maintenance None

Scopes:

Scope Module core terracotta-core modrinth terracotta-provider-modrinth gradle-plugin terracotta-gradle-plugin github terracotta-github docs Documentation ci CI/CD workflows

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
feat(core): add TerracottaVersion.displayName field
fix(modrinth): handle missing project gracefully
refactor(gradle-plugin): simplify provider discovery
docs: fix architecture diagram in README

docs.title: Breaking Changes
docs.text:
Mark with ! suffix or BREAKING CHANGE footer:

feat(core)!: remove deprecated API

feat(core): new API for version management

BREAKING CHANGE: Old API removed

docs.title: Pull Request Requirements
docs.text:
Before a PR can be merged:

  • [ ] Build passes: ./gradlew build
  • [ ] Tests pass: ./gradlew test
  • [ ] Spotless passes: ./gradlew spotlessCheck
  • [ ] Test coverage maintained or improved
  • [ ] Documentation updated (if applicable)

See Contributing How-To for detailed PR process.

docs.title: Python Style
docs.text:
No formal formatter enforced, but scripts should:

  • Follow PEP 8 style guide
  • Use descriptive names
  • Include type hints
  • Be consistent with existing code

docs.title: Documentation Style
docs.text:
• Follow the Documentation Framework for content organization
  • Each page belongs to exactly one type (Tutorial, How-to, Reference, Explanation)
  • Use markdown headers for structure
  • Include code examples where helpful
  • Link to related content

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: This reference covers all configuration files for the Terracotta project.

docs.title: Root Build Configuration (build.gradle.kts)
docs.text:
The root build file defines shared configuration for all subprojects.

Key Configuration:

plugins {
    alias(libs.plugins.kotlin.jvm) apply false
    alias(libs.plugins.kotlin.serialization) apply false
    alias(libs.plugins.spotless)
    alias(libs.plugins.dokka)
    alias(libs.plugins.central.portal.publisher)
    jacoco
}

Settings:

  • All projects share version io.github.beduality:0.1.1
  • Gradle plugins use version catalog from gradle/libs.versions.toml
  • Spotless for code formatting
  • Dokka for API documentation
  • Central Portal Publisher for Maven Central publishing
  • JaCoCo for test coverage

docs.title: Version Catalog (gradle/libs.versions.toml)
docs.text:
The version catalog defines all library versions in a single file.

Available Versions:

Library Version Purpose Kotlin 2.3.21 JVM language Ktor 2.3.12 HTTP client/server kotlinx-serialization 1.7.1 JSON serialization Spotless 6.25.0 Code formatting Dokka 1.9.20 API documentation JUnit 5.10.2 Testing framework Pulumi 1.30.0 Infrastructure as code JaCoCo 0.8.12 Test coverage

Available Plugins:

Plugin Version Purpose kotlin-jvm 2.3.21 Kotlin JVM target kotlin-serialization 2.3.21 Kotlin serialization plugin spotless 6.25.0 Code formatting dokka 1.9.20 API documentation central-portal-publisher 0.2.0-alpha.1 Maven Central publishing via Sonatype Central Portal

docs.title: MkDocs (mkdocs.yml)
docs.text:
The MkDocs configuration defines site structure, theme, and navigation.

Key Settings:

site_name: Terracotta
site_description: Declarative Minecraft project registry management tool
site_url: https://beduality.github.io/terracotta/
repo_url: https://github.com/beduality/terracotta

Theme Configuration:

  • Material theme with custom directory
  • Logo and favicon paths
  • Dark/light palette with deep-purple primary color
  • Features: instant navigation, search, tabs, code copying

Markdown Extensions:

  • pymdownx.highlight: Syntax highlighting with line numbers
  • pymdownx.superfences: Custom code fences (Mermaid diagrams)
  • pymdownx.tabbed: Tabbed content
  • pymdownx.details: Collapsible content blocks
  • admonition: Alert boxes (note, warning, tip)
  • tables: Markdown tables

Plugins:

  • search: Full-text search
  • hooks: Build-time hooks for copying generated docs

Navigation Structure:

  • Quick Start
  • Gradle Plugin
  • SDK
  • Repo

docs.title: Gradle Plugin Module
docs.text:
The Gradle plugin module (modules/terracotta-gradle-plugin) has additional configuration:

Plugin Metadata:

  • ID: io.github.beduality.terracotta
  • Name: Terracotta
  • Description: Declarative Minecraft project registry management
  • Website: Project GitHub repository
  • Version: From gradle/libs.versions.toml

docs.title: Provider Modules
docs.text:
Provider modules (terracotta-provider-modrinth) register their ProviderFactory via ServiceLoader:

META-INF/services/
└── io.github.beduality.terracotta.core.provider.ProviderFactory

The file contains the fully qualified class name of the factory implementation.

docs.title: Build Environment
docs.text: Variable Purpose Required JAVA_HOME JDK 21 path Yes (if system default differs)

docs.title: Release Environment
docs.text: Variable Purpose Required SONATYPE_USERNAME Sonatype Central Portal username Yes (for releases) SONATYPE_PASSWORD Sonatype Central Portal password/token Yes (for releases) SIGNING_KEY GPG private key (ASCII-armored) Yes (for releases) SIGNING_PASSWORD GPG key passphrase Yes (for releases)

docs.title: API Tokens
docs.text: Variable Purpose Provider MODRINTH_TOKEN Modrinth API token Modrinth

docs.title: File Structure Reference
docs.text:
.
├── docs/                   # Documentation (MkDocs)
│   ├── content/            # Content organized by topic (sdk, gradle-plugin, repo)
│   ├── hooks/              # Build hooks for copying generated docs
│   └── overrides/          # Theme customizations
├── guidelines/             # Contribution guidelines
├── modules/                # Gradle modules
├── project/                # Project management (TODO, BACKLOG)
├── scripts/                # Utility scripts (release, deployment)
├── build.gradle.kts        # Root build configuration
├── settings.gradle.kts     # Gradle settings
└── mkdocs.yml              # Documentation configuration

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
• terracotta-core: Pure domain library with canonical models, provider interfaces, and semantic diff engine. Published to Maven Central as io.github.beduality:terracotta-core.
  • terracotta-provider-modrinth: Modrinth state and registry providers using Ktor Client and Kotlinx Serialization.
  • terracotta-gradle-plugin: Gradle plugin providing terracottaPlan and terracottaApply tasks with multi-provider support via ServiceLoader.
  • terracotta-github: Pulumi infrastructure (Kotlin + Java SDK) for managing GitHub repository settings and Action secrets.

docs.title: Kotlin / JVM
docs.text:
The main codebase is written in Kotlin 2.3.21 targeting JVM 17, compiled under JDK 21.

Concern Tool How to run Dependency management Gradle with version catalog (gradle/libs.versions.toml) JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew build Formatting & linting ktlint via Spotless 6.25.0 JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew spotlessCheck Auto-fix formatting ktlint via Spotless JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew spotlessApply API docs Dokka 1.9.20 JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew dokkaHtml Testing JUnit 5.10.2 JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk ./gradlew test

Kotlin does not have a separate type-checker step — type errors surface during compilation (./gradlew build).

Spotless must pass before a PR can be merged. Run spotlessApply to auto-fix formatting rather than fixing it by hand.

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
Python is used for the release process automation and documentation tooling. The minimum required version is Python 3.13. Dependencies are managed with uv.

Concern Tool Dependency management uv Testing pytest

There is no enforced formatter or linter for Python scripts at this time. Keep scripts readable and consistent with the existing style.

docs.title: Navigating the Codebase
docs.text: This tutorial will help you understand the Terracotta project structure and get started contributing.

docs.title: What you'll learn
docs.text:
• How to clone and set up the repository locally
  • The project's Git workflow and branch strategy
  • How project management works with TODO, BACKLOG, and proposals
  • How to navigate the multi-module Gradle structure

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Git 2.54.0 or later
  • JDK 21 for building
  • Python 3.13+ for documentation (optional)

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
Fork the repository on GitHub, then clone your fork locally:

git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/terracotta.git
cd terracotta

Add the upstream remote to pull in future changes:

git remote add upstream https://github.com/beduality/terracotta.git

docs.title: 2. Understand the Branch Strategy
docs.text:
The project uses two main branches:

  • main: The development branch with the latest code and documentation
  • project: A separate branch used for project management (TODO, BACKLOG, proposals)

The project branch is checked out as a git worktree at project/ from the main branch. This keeps planning separate from code while maintaining version history.

docs.title: 3. Project Management Workflow
docs.text: All project planning happens through Pull Requests against the project branch. There are no external issue trackers.

docs.title: TODO
docs.text:
TODO.md contains concrete, actionable tasks ready to be worked on. To pick up a task:

  1. Open a PR against the project branch
  2. Remove the task from TODO.md in that PR
  3. Wait for merge before starting implementation

docs.title: Backlog
docs.text: BACKLOG.md contains ideas and tasks not yet ready for implementation. Move items to TODO when they become well-defined and actionable.

docs.title: Proposals
docs.text:
For larger changes, create a proposal in project/proposals/:

  1. Create a Markdown file with a descriptive name
  2. Include: problem, solution, alternatives, open questions
  3. Open a PR against project containing only the proposal
  4. Iterate through review until accepted or rejected

docs.title: 4. Module Structure
docs.text:
The project is organized as a multi-module Gradle build under modules/:

modules/
├── terracotta-core/              # Pure domain library
├── terracotta-provider-modrinth/ # Modrinth registry integration
├── terracotta-gradle-plugin/     # Gradle plugin frontend
└── terracotta-github/            # Pulumi infrastructure

docs.title: terracotta-core
docs.text: Contains canonical models, provider interfaces, and the semantic diff engine. Depends only on standard Kotlin/Java APIs - no network or framework dependencies.

docs.title: terracotta-provider-modrinth
docs.text: Implements registry-specific behaviors for Modrinth. Depends on Ktor Client and Kotlinx Serialization.

docs.title: terracotta-gradle-plugin
docs.text: Build-tool integration providing terracottaPlan and terracottaApply tasks. Uses ServiceLoader for provider discovery.

docs.title: terracotta-github
docs.text: Manages GitHub repository configuration and secrets using Pulumi Java/Kotlin.

docs.title: 5. Next Steps
docs.text:
• Building and Testing: Learn how to compile and test
  • Testing Guide: Understand test types and coverage
  • Contributing: Review contribution guidelines

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This tutorial will get you up and running with Terracotta in under 10 minutes.

docs.title: What you'll learn
docs.text:
• How to clone and set up the repository locally
  • How to build the project for the first time
  • How to run tests to verify your setup
  • How to start making changes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Git 2.54.0 or later
  • JDK 21
  • Python 3.13+ (optional, for documentation)

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
Fork the repository on GitHub, then clone your fork locally:

git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/terracotta.git
cd terracotta

Add the upstream remote to pull in future changes:

git remote add upstream https://github.com/beduality/terracotta.git

docs.title: 2. Verify Your Java Setup
docs.text:
Terracotta requires JDK 21. Verify your setup:

java -version
# Should show openjdk version "21.x.x"

If your system default is a newer Java version, prefix commands with:

export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk

docs.title: 3. Build the Project
docs.text:
Run the complete build pipeline to verify everything works:

export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk
./gradlew build

This command:

  • Compiles all modules
  • Runs all unit and integration tests
  • Checks code formatting
  • Generates coverage reports

If the build succeeds, you're ready to contribute!

docs.title: 4. Run Tests
docs.text:
Verify tests pass independently:

export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk
./gradlew test

docs.title: 5. Format Your Code
docs.text:
Before committing, ensure your code follows the project style:

export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk
./gradlew spotlessApply

docs.title: 6. Next Steps
docs.text:
• Navigating the Codebase: Understand the project structure in detail
  • Building and Testing: Learn about build options and verification
  • Testing Guide: Understand test types and coverage expectations

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text:
You now have a working local copy of Terracotta. Ready to make your first contribution?

  1. Pick a task from project/TODO.md
  2. Follow the Contributing guide
  3. Create your PR against the main branch

docs.title: Writing Changelog Entries
docs.text: This tutorial teaches how to write changelog entries that are clear, useful, and follow project conventions.

docs.title: What is a Changelog?
docs.text: A changelog is a curated, structured record of notable changes made to a project. It helps users, developers, and operators understand what has changed between versions.

docs.title: Changelog Format
docs.text:
The changelog follows the Keep a Changelog format and is organized by:

  1. Category - Added, Changed, Fixed, Deprecated, Removed, Security
  2. Module - Docs, Core, Gradle Plugin, Modrinth, SDK
  3. Audience - Players, Server Admins, Developers, Maintainers (optional)

Example structure:

## [Unreleased]

### Added
#### Core
- New feature for X

### Changed
#### Gradle Plugin
- Updated Y to improve Z

### Fixed
#### Modrinth
- Bug fix for ABC

docs.title: 1. Focus on Impact, Not Implementation
docs.text:
Good:

  • "Added support for Minecraft 1.21"
  • "Fixed issue with version uploads failing"

Bad:

  • "Refactored VersionService to use VersionHandler"
  • "Updated dependency from OkHttp 4.9 to 4.12"

docs.title: 2. Be Specific and Concrete
docs.text:
Good:

  • " terracottaPlan now shows ~ for metadata updates"
  • "Added TerracottaEnvironment.CLIENT_ONLY support"

Bad:

  • "Improved the plan output"
  • "Added new environment option"

docs.title: 3. Include "Why" When Helpful
docs.text:
Add a - **Why**: line if the motivation isn't obvious:

### Added
#### Core
- `TerracottaVersion.displayName` field
  - **Why**: Support user-defined version names instead of auto-generated ones

docs.title: 4. Group Related Changes
docs.text:
If multiple changes relate to the same feature, group them:

### Added
#### Gradle Plugin
- `terracottaPlanModrinth` and `terracottaApplyModrinth` tasks
  - **Why**: Allow publishing to Modrinth without affecting other registries
- `terracottaPlanHangar` and `terracottaApplyHangar` tasks
  - **Why**: Allow publishing to Hangar without affecting other registries

docs.title: Step 1: Identify the Change
docs.text:
Review your PR and identify what changed from a user's perspective:

  • Did you add a new feature?
  • Did you fix a bug that users might encounter?
  • Did you change behavior or API?

docs.title: Step 2: Determine the Category
docs.text:
Choose the most appropriate category:

Category When to Use Added New functionality Changed Existing behavior modified Fixed Bug fixes Deprecated Feature marked for removal Removed Feature deleted Security Security-related fixes

docs.title: Step 3: Determine the Module
docs.text:
Choose the module that contains the changed code:

  • Docs - Documentation changes
  • Core - terracotta-core module
  • Gradle Plugin - terracotta-gradle-plugin module
  • Modrinth - terracotta-provider-modrinth module
  • SDK - SDK-related changes

docs.title: Step 4: Write the Entry
docs.text:
Follow the format:

### Category
#### Module
- Change description
  - **Why**: (optional) Reason for the change

docs.title: Step 5: Place It in the Changelog
docs.text: Add the entry under ## [Unreleased] in CHANGELOG.md, maintaining alphabetical order within categories and modules.

docs.title: Changelog Guidelines Reference
docs.text: For more detailed guidelines, see the Changelog Guidelines.

docs.title: Breaking Changes
docs.text:
Mark clearly as breaking: 

### Changed
#### Core
- `TerracottaVersion` fields are now required
  - **Breaking**: Versions without required fields will fail validation
  - **Migration**: Add all required fields to your version definitions

docs.title: API Changes
docs.text:
Document API changes:

### Changed
#### SDK
- `DiffEngine.diff()` now returns `List<Operation>` instead of `OperationGroup`
  - **API**: The return type changed to simplify operation handling
  - **Migration**: Update your code to handle `List<Operation>` directly

docs.title: Performance Improvements
docs.text:
### Changed
#### Core
- Version comparison is now 3x faster
  - **Why**: Improved diff algorithm using tree-based comparison

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: Terracotta follows clean code and modular design principles, isolating the core domain logic from the specific registry adapters (e.g. Modrinth) and the build-tool integration (Gradle plugin).

docs.title: Layer & Module Overview
docs.text:
graph TD
    subgraph terracotta-gradle-plugin["terracotta-gradle-plugin (Gradle Plugin)"]
        Plugin["TerracottaPlugin"]
        PlanTask["TerracottaPlanTask"]
        ApplyTask["TerracottaApplyTask"]
        ServiceLoaderLookup["ServiceLoader Discovery"]
    end

    subgraph terracotta-provider-modrinth["terracotta-provider-modrinth (Adapter)"]
        ModrinthClient["ModrinthClient"]
        ModrinthState["ModrinthStateProvider"]
        ModrinthRegistry["ModrinthRegistryProvider"]
        ProviderFactoryImpl["ModrinthProviderFactory"]
    end

    subgraph terracotta-core["terracotta-core (Core SDK)"]
        DiffEngine["DiffEngine"]
        StateProvider["StateProvider (Interface)"]
        RegistryProvider["RegistryProvider (Interface)"]
        ProviderFactory["ProviderFactory (Interface)"]
